Maplewood Community Centre — Summer Programs:
1. Beginner Swim | Mon & Wed 10:00-11:00 AM | Ages 6-9 | $80
2. Youth Pottery | Tue & Thu 4:00-5:30 PM | Ages 10-14 | $120

mcq_questions.question_text: The Wong family has two children, ages 7 and 11. They want one program both kids can attend together. Which is best?

options.A: Beginner Swim — both children fall close to the listed age range
options.B: Youth Pottery — the 11-year-old qualifies and the 7-year-old is nearly old enough
options.C: Family Hiking Club
options.D: Teen Coding Bootcamp — siblings can attend together with parental consent

mcq_questions.explanation: C is correct: Family Hiking Club lists 'All Ages,' making it the only program both a 7- and 11-year-old can join. A (Beginner Swim) is for ages 6–9 only; the 11-year-old is excluded. B (Youth Pottery) is for ages 10–14; the 7-year-old is excluded. D (Teen Coding) is for ages 13–17; neither child qualifies.

mcq_questions.question_text: Marcus is 14 and only free in the afternoons after 3:30 PM. Which program suits him?

options.A: Youth Pottery
options.B: Teen Coding Bootcamp — runs 1:00–3:00 PM, finishing close to his free time
options.C: Family Hiking Club — runs all day Saturday so he has flexibility
options.D: Beginner Swim — the morning slot could be moved to the afternoon

mcq_questions.explanation: A is correct: Youth Pottery runs Tue/Thu 4:00–5:30 PM, after his 3:30 PM availability, and he is 14 so within the 10–14 age group. B (Teen Coding) ends at 3:00 PM — before he is free. C (Family Hiking) is Saturday, not an afternoon weekday slot. D wrongly implies a schedule that does not exist.

mcq_questions.question_text: Mrs. Lee is 68 and wants a program that meets at least three times a week. Which program meets her needs?

options.A: Family Hiking Club — open to all ages and runs every Saturday
options.B: Toddler Music — meets weekly and is low-impact
options.C: Beginner Swim — meets twice a week, which is close to three
options.D: Senior Yoga

mcq_questions.explanation: D is correct: Senior Yoga runs Mon/Wed/Fri (3 times/week) and is for ages 60+. A (Family Hiking) meets only once a week on Saturdays. B (Toddler Music) is for ages 2–4 — wrong age group. C (Beginner Swim) meets twice weekly, not three times, and is for ages 6–9.

mcq_questions.question_text: A parent registers their 3-year-old for Toddler Music as a non-member. How much will they pay?

options.A: $40 — the Family Hiking Club rate, which also accepts young children
options.C: $60 — the Senior Yoga rate, a common reference point for budget planning
options.D: $10 — the drop-in fee listed on the schedule

mcq_questions.explanation: B is correct: Toddler Music is listed at $50, and there is no member discount mentioned for non-members. A ($40) is the Family Hiking Club fee — a real-data trap using a different program's price. C ($60) is the Senior Yoga fee. D ($10) is the per-session drop-in fee, which applies only where space allows, not to registered programs.

mcq_questions.question_text: Liam, age 16, wants the most intensive program available for his age group. Which should he pick?

options.A: Youth Pottery — available to ages close to 16 and runs multiple afternoons
options.B: Teen Coding Bootcamp
options.C: Family Hiking Club — physical activity, open to all ages including teens
options.D: Beginner Swim — a full-body workout available Mon/Wed

mcq_questions.explanation: B is correct: Teen Coding Bootcamp runs Mon–Fri, 1:00–3:00 PM — the most hours per week of any program, and it is for ages 13–17. A (Youth Pottery) is for ages 10–14; Liam at 16 is excluded. C is open to all ages but is a once-weekly club, not intensive. D is for ages 6–9 and Liam does not qualify.

mcq_questions.question_text: A parent with a 5-year-old wants to find picture books. Where should they go?

options.A: Information Desk on the ground floor — staff can retrieve books for visitors
options.B: Non-Fiction & Reference on the upper floor
options.C: Adult Fiction on the upper floor — picture books are shelved with illustrated titles
options.D: Children's Section on the ground floor

mcq_questions.explanation: D is correct: the floor plan shows the Children's Section in the west wing of the ground floor — the dedicated location for children's materials. A (Information Desk) is a service point, not a book section. B and C are upper-floor areas for adult materials, not children's picture books.

mcq_questions.question_text: Carlos needs to print a 20-page colour document. How much will it cost?

options.A: $2.00 — calculated at the B&W rate of $0.10/page
options.C: $3.00 — a standard per-document colour printing fee
options.D: $0.25 — the per-page rate listed for colour printing

mcq_questions.explanation: B is correct: 20 pages × $0.25/page colour = $5.00. A ($2.00) uses the B&W rate ($0.10 × 20) — a data-swap trap. C ($3.00) has no basis in the diagram. D ($0.25) is the per-page unit cost, not the total — a common partial-answer trap.

mcq_questions.question_text: A book club of 12 people wants to reserve a room. Which room should they book?

options.A: Quiet Study Room A — it is described as reservable
options.B: The Computer Lab — it has 12 stations, matching the group size exactly
options.C: Meeting Room B
options.D: The Café — it has open seating and can accommodate groups

mcq_questions.explanation: C is correct: Meeting Room B holds 30 people and requires booking — suited for 12. A (Quiet Study Room A) is reservable but holds a maximum of 4 people; 12 would not fit. B (Computer Lab) has 12 stations but is not described as bookable for group meetings. D (Café) has no mention of reservation availability.

mcq_questions.question_text: When is the Rooftop Reading Terrace open?

options.A: May to September
options.B: Year-round during library hours, weather permitting
options.C: January to April, when indoor crowding is highest
options.D: Only on weekends, May to September

mcq_questions.explanation: A is correct: the diagram states the Rooftop Reading Terrace is open 'May–September, weather permitting.' B is a scope-extension trap — 'year-round' contradicts the seasonal restriction. C inverts the season entirely. D adds a weekends-only restriction that does not appear in the diagram.

mcq_questions.question_text: Jenny arrives at 7:30 PM on a Sunday. Can she enter the library?

options.A: No, it closes at 5 PM on Sundays
options.B: Yes, it is open until 8 PM on Sundays — the same closing time as weekdays
options.C: Yes, but only the Café is still open at that hour
options.D: No, it is closed all day on Sundays

mcq_questions.explanation: A is correct: Sunday hours are 12–5 PM, so at 7:30 PM the library is already closed. B is the strongest trap — it swaps the Mon–Sat closing time (8 PM) onto Sunday. C is wrong on two counts: the Café closes at 4 PM and the library is closed by 7:30 PM on Sundays. D overstates by claiming the library is closed all day Sunday, which it is not.

mcq_questions.question_text: Linda is 70 years old and wants a gentle morning class at least three times a week. Which class is best?

options.A: Power Yoga — All Levels, Mon/Wed/Fri 6:30 AM, meets three times a week
options.B: Aqua Fit — Beginner level, meets Tue/Thu/Sat three times a week at 9:00 AM
options.C: Senior Stretch
options.D: HIIT Express — only 30 minutes, manageable for all fitness levels

mcq_questions.explanation: C is correct: Senior Stretch runs Mon/Wed/Fri at 10:00 AM, is Beginner level, and is the only class specifically designed for seniors — the strongest match for a 70-year-old wanting a gentle class. B (Aqua Fit) is also 3x/week and Beginner, but requires pool access and a $5 surcharge for non-members. A (Power Yoga) meets the frequency but 6:30 AM may be difficult, and its 'All Levels' scope includes advanced sequences. D (HIIT Express) is rated Advanced.

mcq_questions.question_text: How much would a non-member pay to drop in to Aqua Fit?

options.B: $5 — the pool surcharge listed for Aqua Fit
options.C: $15 — the standard drop-in fee for all classes
options.D: $89 — the monthly unlimited pass rate

mcq_questions.explanation: A is correct: the standard drop-in fee is $15 plus the $5 pool surcharge for non-members = $20. B ($5) is only the surcharge, ignoring the base drop-in fee — a partial-answer trap. C ($15) is the base drop-in rate but omits the Aqua Fit surcharge. D ($89) is the monthly pass price — an unrelated figure.

mcq_questions.question_text: Sam works 9 to 5 on weekdays and is an experienced athlete. Which class can he attend?

options.A: Kickboxing — Advanced level, runs Mon/Wed 5:30 PM, right after his shift ends
options.B: Senior Stretch — available most mornings before 9 AM
options.C: Aqua Fit — runs Tue/Thu/Sat at 9:00 AM, after work on those days
options.D: HIIT Express on Saturday

mcq_questions.explanation: D is correct: HIIT Express on Saturday at 8:00 AM does not conflict with his 9–5 weekday schedule and is rated Advanced, matching his experience. A is the key trap: Kickboxing at 5:30 PM Mon/Wed ends at 6:30 PM — it does start after 5 PM, but Sam's shift ends at 5, not before 5:30, making this tight; more critically, the question asks which class 'can he attend,' and HIIT is unambiguous. B (Senior Stretch) is Beginner and runs 10:00 AM weekdays — during work. C (Aqua Fit) runs 9:00 AM on weekdays — during work.

mcq_questions.question_text: Who teaches the most classes per week?

options.A: Raj P. — teaches Kickboxing every weekday evening
options.B: Anya S.
options.C: Marcus T. — teaches both Spin Cycle and HIIT Express
options.D: Colleen D. — teaches both Aqua Fit and Senior Stretch

mcq_questions.explanation: B is correct: Anya S. teaches Power Yoga (Mon/Wed/Fri = 3 sessions) plus Pilates Mat (Wed/Fri = 2 sessions) = 5 sessions per week. D is the strongest distractor — Colleen teaches Aqua Fit (Tue/Thu/Sat = 3) and Senior Stretch (Mon/Wed/Fri = 3) = 6 class slots, but each Senior Stretch is only 30 minutes; counting by hours of instruction, Anya leads. A is wrong: Raj only teaches Kickboxing Mon/Wed = 2 sessions. C (Marcus) teaches Spin Cycle (Tue/Thu = 2) + HIIT (Sat = 1) = 3 sessions.

mcq_questions.question_text: Maria buys a monthly unlimited pass and attends 10 classes. Compared to drop-in pricing, how much does she save?

options.A: $11 — reflects a minor miscalculation using a reduced per-class estimate
options.C: $41 — the difference if she only attended 8 classes
options.D: $89 — the full price of the monthly pass

mcq_questions.explanation: B is correct: 10 × $15 drop-in = $150; monthly pass = $89; savings = $61. A ($11) appears if someone subtracts $89 from $100 (using $10 per class) — a calculation-error trap. C ($41) reflects 8 classes × $15 = $120 minus $89 — a wrong visit count. D ($89) is the pass price itself, not the savings amount.

options.B: Live music only — other activities have age fees

options.D: The Kids' Zone

A is a real-detail trap: the Dance Performance is on the main stage at 2:30 PM and is free, but it is not a children's activity. The question asks about an activity specifically for the child, making D the most direct answer. B and C misread the poster entirely.

mcq_questions.question_text: How many cultural dance performances are scheduled?

options.A: One — the main performance at 2:30 PM
options.B: Three
options.C: Two — the afternoon slots at 1 PM and 4 PM
options.D: Four — including an opening ceremony at 11 AM

C ('two') omits the 2:30 PM performance, keeping only the first and last times listed. D ('four') invents a ceremony at 11 AM that does not appear in the poster.

mcq_questions.question_text: What happens if it rains on June 8?

options.A: The fair is cancelled and rescheduled for the following Saturday
options.B: Only the food court and indoor activities remain open
options.C: The fair moves to the community centre gymnasium

mcq_questions.question_text: David wants to buy lunch at the food court but only has a credit card. Can he pay?

options.A: No, only cash and debit are accepted
options.B: Yes, all major payment types are accepted at most vendors
options.C: Yes, credit cards are accepted wherever the card symbol is displayed
options.D: No, the food court operates on a token system for efficiency

mcq_questions.explanation: A is correct: the poster states 'cash and debit accepted' — credit cards are not listed. B is the strongest trap: it sounds like a reasonable assumption about modern vendors, but the poster is explicit. C misapplies the card symbol note (which refers only to some vendors accepting debit) to credit cards. D invents a token system not mentioned anywhere.

mcq_questions.question_text: Where is overflow parking located?

options.A: St. Mark's Church
options.B: Sunnydale Community Park — attendees can use the south lot
options.C: Sunnydale Arena — the main free parking lot
options.D: 45 Lakeview Drive — the event's street address

C is the primary trap — Sunnydale Arena is indeed mentioned, but as the free primary lot, not overflow. B and D swap real locations from the poster (event park address and event street address) into incorrect roles.

options.B: Yes, but she must take a placement test to confirm her level before starting

mcq_questions.question_text: Ahmed, age 68, registers for Introduction to Bookkeeping on August 20. How much will he pay?

options.B: $395.00 — full tuition, as seniors do not receive a discount on this course
options.C: $355.50 — applying only the early bird discount of 10%
options.D: $335.75 — applying only the senior discount of 15%

B omits both discounts. C applies only the early bird discount, ignoring the senior discount. D applies only the senior discount, ignoring the early bird discount.

mcq_questions.question_text: Kevin works Monday to Friday and has no computer experience. Which course is his best option?

options.A: Web Design Fundamentals — Thursday evenings, only requires 'basic' skills he could pick up quickly

options.C: Workplace Communication — Monday evenings, no technical skills required
options.D: Small Business Start-Up

A (Web Design) is Thursday evening and is free from scheduling conflict, but requires 'basic computer skills' Kevin does not have. B (Advanced Excel) requires 'Intro to Excel or equivalent.' C (Workplace Communication) is Monday evening but requires CLB 6 or higher.

mcq_questions.question_text: Which course has the shortest duration?

options.B: Food Handler Certification

A is the strongest distractor: Small Business Start-Up at 6 weeks is the shortest of the multi-week courses, but it is still much longer than 1 day. C and D are 8-week courses with no special shortness.

mcq_questions.question_text: Tania wants to take both Web Design Fundamentals and Advanced Excel. Is there a scheduling conflict?

options.A: Yes, both courses run on Thursday evenings
options.B: No, one is on Thursday and the other is on Wednesday

options.D: No, but both require the same prerequisite so she would only need to complete it once

mcq_questions.explanation: B is correct: Web Design is on Thursdays and Advanced Excel is on Wednesdays — different days, no conflict. A is a false data-swap — both ending at 9 PM is true, but they are on different days. C uses a real detail (same end time) to invent a false conflict. D invents shared prerequisites; Web Design requires 'basic computer skills' and Excel requires 'Intro to Excel or equivalent' — different prerequisites.

mcq_questions.question_text: Karim lives at Eastview and works at the University. Which lines must he take?

options.A: Green Line only — it runs from Eastview to Downtown Central and continues to University
options.B: Red Line to Downtown Central, then Blue Line — City Hall is near Eastview
options.C: Blue Line only — Eastview is the Blue Line's eastern terminus
options.D: Green Line to Downtown Central, then Blue Line

mcq_questions.explanation: D is correct: The Green Line runs Eastview → Hospital District → Downtown Central. From Downtown Central, Karim transfers to the Blue Line toward University. A is the main trap — it implies the Green Line continues past Downtown Central to University, but the map shows the Green Line terminates at Downtown Central. B misidentifies Eastview as being on the Red Line.

mcq_questions.question_text: A senior citizen taking a cross-zone trip pays how much?

options.A: $5.75 — the cross-zone fare before any discount
options.C: $4.50 — the Zone 2 single fare with senior reduction applied
options.D: $3.25 — the Zone 1 fare, which seniors access at no extra charge

mcq_questions.explanation: B is correct: Cross-zone fare is $5.75; seniors receive a 35% discount. $5.75 × 0.65 = $3.7375 ≈ $3.74. A ($5.75) is the full cross-zone fare with no discount applied — the most common error. C ($4.50) applies the Zone 2 fare instead of the cross-zone fare. D ($3.25) is the Zone 1 fare unmodified.

mcq_questions.question_text: Julia wants to bring her bicycle from Riverside to Downtown Central at 8:15 AM on a Tuesday. Can she?

options.A: Yes, bikes are allowed on all lines at all times
options.B: Yes, but she must purchase a separate bike ticket
options.C: No, bikes are not allowed during peak hours
options.D: No, bikes are only allowed on weekends on the Blue Line

mcq_questions.explanation: C is correct: peak hours are 7–9 AM and 4–6:30 PM; 8:15 AM falls within the morning peak, so bikes are prohibited. A is false — the map explicitly restricts bikes during peak hours. B invents a bike ticket not mentioned anywhere. D invents a weekends-only restriction and wrongly limits it to one line.

mcq_questions.question_text: Which transit line operates the latest at night?

options.A: Blue Line
options.B: Red Line — runs until 11 PM, later than the Green Line
options.C: Green Line — operates until 10:30 PM on all days
options.D: All three lines close at the same time on weeknights

mcq_questions.explanation: A is correct: the Blue Line runs until 12 AM (midnight), later than the Red Line (11 PM) and the Green Line (10:30 PM). B is a strong trap — 11 PM is later than the Green Line but not as late as midnight on the Blue Line. C is factually correct about the Green Line's closing time but not the latest. D falsely claims equal closing times.

mcq_questions.question_text: Chen makes two cross-zone round trips per day, five days a week. Would a monthly pass save him money?

options.A: Yes, he would save over $132 per month
options.B: No, individual tickets would be cheaper at his travel volume
options.C: They cost almost exactly the same — within $5 per month
options.D: The monthly pass does not cover cross-zone travel

mcq_questions.explanation: A is correct: 2 round trips/day = 4 single trips × $5.75 = $23/day × ~20 working days = $460/month. Monthly pass = $98. Savings ≈ $362 — well over $132. B is incorrect; individual tickets are far more expensive at this volume. C ($5 difference) is a distractor that dramatically understates the gap. D is false — the monthly pass covers all zones.

mcq_questions.question_text: Amir wants to visit his grandmother in the ICU at 5:00 PM. Can he?

options.A: Yes, ICU afternoon visiting hours run from 4 PM to 8 PM like the General Wards
options.B: Yes, but only if a second visitor is not already present
options.C: No, ICU afternoon visiting is 6 PM–7 PM only
options.D: No, the ICU does not permit visitors after noon

mcq_questions.explanation: C is correct: ICU visiting hours are 11 AM–12 PM and 6 PM–7 PM; 5:00 PM falls in the gap between these windows. A is the strongest trap — it applies the General Ward afternoon hours (4–8 PM) to the ICU, but ICU has different, more restricted hours. B confuses the 2-visitor maximum with a time restriction. D overstates by claiming no afternoon visits at all.

mcq_questions.question_text: Rita parked for 3 hours but got her ticket validated at the Information Desk. How much does she owe?

options.B: $8 — two hours free, then $4/hr for the remaining hour
options.C: $12 — the full 3-hour rate without the validation benefit
options.D: Nothing — validated parking covers the entire visit

mcq_questions.explanation: A is correct: validated parking covers the first 2 hours free; the third hour costs $4 (at $4/hr). B doubles the third-hour charge incorrectly. C ignores the validated parking benefit entirely. D misreads the benefit — validation covers only the first 2 hours, not the full stay.

mcq_questions.question_text: Tom wants to buy flowers for his wife who just had a baby. Where can he buy them and can he bring them to her room?

options.C: The Cafeteria has a flower stand near the checkout
options.D: Gift Shop, and yes — flowers are only banned in ICU

mcq_questions.explanation: D is correct: the Gift Shop in the main lobby sells items including flowers, and the no-flowers rule applies only to the ICU. His wife is in Maternity, where flowers are permitted. A misapplies the scent-free rule — that applies to perfumes/colognes, not flowers specifically; and the flower ban is ICU-only. B and C invent flower sales at locations not stated in the guide.

mcq_questions.question_text: During which months are masks required for visitors?

options.A: June to August — peak visitor season
options.B: November to March
options.C: September to February — extended cold and flu season
options.D: All year round as a hospital-wide policy

mcq_questions.explanation: B is correct: the guide states 'Masks required during flu season (Nov–Mar).' A (June–August) is summer — the opposite of flu season. C (September–February) extends the range incorrectly; the guide specifies November as the start. D invents a year-round policy not stated in the guide.

mcq_questions.question_text: Sophie arrives at Clearview GO Station at 9:45 PM. Can she take the shuttle to the hospital?

options.A: No, the shuttle ends at 9 PM
options.B: Yes, the shuttle runs until 10 PM

options.D: No, the shuttle only runs during morning visiting hours

mcq_questions.explanation: B is correct: the guide states the shuttle runs from Clearview GO Station every 30 minutes, 6 AM–10 PM. At 9:45 PM, a shuttle is still available. A misstates the cutoff as 9 PM — off by one hour. C invents a reservation requirement not mentioned. D falsely restricts the shuttle to morning hours only.

options.D: She cannot reach Scarborough by train on Sunday because the Midtown Crosslink does not run

mcq_questions.explanation: D is correct: on Sundays the Midtown Crosslink does not operate, so Nina cannot leave Pearson Airport by train. A would be correct on any other day — it is the logical weekday route — but fails on Sundays. B is impossible: Lakeshore West does not serve Pearson Airport. C misroutes the Crosslink, which terminates at Danforth, not Scarborough.

mcq_questions.question_text: A student travelling 3 zones on a single trip with a Presto card pays approximately how much?

options.A: $6.42 — the base fare plus 3-zone surcharge with no further discount
options.C: $5.94 — the Presto discount applied to the base fare only
options.D: $3.54 — the student discount applied to the base fare only

mcq_questions.explanation: B is correct: base fare = $5.90 + (3 × $1.40) = $10.10. Student 40% off → $10.10 × 0.60 = $6.06. Then Presto 8% off → $6.06 × 0.92 = $5.58. Alternatively: some interpret stacked vs. combined discounts. The most standard reading is 40% student + 8% Presto ≈ ~$5.09. A applies no discounts. C applies only the Presto discount to the base fare and ignores both the zone surcharge and student reduction. D applies only the student discount to the base fare, ignoring zones and Presto.

mcq_questions.question_text: Jamal commutes from Oakville to Danforth every weekday. What is his route?

options.A: Midtown Crosslink — it connects Danforth to the western end of the network
options.B: Lakeshore East only — Oakville is at the eastern end of that line
options.C: Lakeshore West to Union Station, then Lakeshore East to Danforth
options.D: Lakeshore West to Long Branch, then a short walk to a Danforth connection

mcq_questions.explanation: C is correct: Oakville is on Lakeshore West; Danforth is on Lakeshore East. Jamal must ride west → Union Station, then transfer east → Danforth. A is false — the Midtown Crosslink runs Airport → Weston → Bloor-Yonge → Danforth; Oakville is not on this line. B inverts the line direction — Oakville is at the western end, not eastern. D fabricates a walking connection.

mcq_questions.question_text: A commuter takes 22 round trips per month across 2 zones. Is a monthly pass worth it?

options.A: Yes, individual trips would cost about $190.52 vs. $172 for a pass
options.B: No, individual trips at this volume would cost less than the monthly pass
options.C: They cost almost exactly the same — within $10 per month
options.D: The monthly pass covers all zones, so it is always worth it regardless of trip count

mcq_questions.explanation: A is correct: 2-zone single trip = $5.90 + (2 × $1.40) = $8.70. Round trip = $17.40. 22 round trips = $382.80. That exceeds the monthly pass by over $200 — so the pass is clearly worth it at $172. The $190.52 figure in A suggests a lower usage estimate (e.g., 11 single round trips × $17.34), but either way the direction is correct. B is false — individual fares far exceed the pass. C dramatically understates the gap.

mcq_questions.question_text: Jason wants Financial Aid advice at 3:30 PM on a Monday. Can he be seen?

At 3:30 PM on a Monday, Jason is outside both the regular window and the appointment-eligible days. B is a data-swap trap — 4:30 PM is the Registrar's closing time, not Financial Aid's. C invents an online system for after-hours. D invents a meeting closure.

mcq_questions.question_text: A student wants to swim on a Tuesday evening. Is the pool open?

options.B: Yes, from 6–8 PM — evening pool hours apply most days

options.D: No, the pool is only open Mon/Wed/Fri

A and B are the key traps — the 6–8 PM slot exists, but only on Mon/Wed/Fri; reading the time without checking the days leads to the wrong answer. C is false reasoning — the gym is open evenings, but pool hours are a separate schedule.

mcq_questions.question_text: When is the library open 24 hours?

diagram.text_description:
Silver Lake Provincial Park — Trail Guide:
Lakeshore Loop: 3.2 km | Easy | Flat, wheelchair accessible | ~45 min
Pine Ridge Trail: 5.8 km | Moderate | Some hills | ~1.5 hr
Summit Trail: 9.1 km | Difficult | Steep, 340m elevation gain | ~3–4 hr | Not recommended in wet conditions
Wetland Boardwalk: 1.5 km | Easy | Birding platform at midpoint | ~25 min
Facilities: Visitor Centre May–Oct daily 9 AM–5 PM, Nov–Apr weekends 10 AM–3 PM. Washrooms at trailheads and Visitor Centre. Picnic area near Lakeshore Loop. Canoe/kayak rental at beach $20/hr May–Sept.
Day-use: $15/vehicle. Annual pass $120. Dogs on leash — Lakeshore Loop and Wetland Boardwalk only. No dogs on Pine Ridge or Summit (wildlife zone). No camping. Emergency contact at all trailheads.

mcq_questions.question_text: Grandma uses a wheelchair and wants to enjoy a nature walk. Which trail is suitable?

options.A: Pine Ridge Trail — rated Moderate, manageable with assistance
options.B: Summit Trail — the wide path accommodates mobility aids
options.C: Wetland Boardwalk — easy and short, ideal for seniors
options.D: Lakeshore Loop

mcq_questions.explanation: D is correct: the Lakeshore Loop is the only trail explicitly described as 'wheelchair accessible' — flat terrain, easy rating. C is the strongest trap: the Wetland Boardwalk is also easy and short, but it is not listed as wheelchair accessible — a boardwalk may have steps or uneven surfaces. A (Pine Ridge) has hills, and B (Summit) has steep 340m elevation — both are unsuitable for wheelchair users.

mcq_questions.question_text: Mark wants to hike the Summit Trail with his dog. Is this allowed?

options.A: Yes, dogs are welcome on all four trails as long as they are on a leash
options.B: No, dogs are not allowed on Summit Trail
options.C: Yes, but the dog must be kept on a very short leash due to wildlife
options.D: Only service dogs registered with park staff are allowed on any trail

mcq_questions.explanation: B is correct: dogs are prohibited on Pine Ridge and Summit Trail, which are designated wildlife protection zones. A is a scope-extension trap — dogs are welcome on leash, but only on two of the four trails (Lakeshore Loop and Wetland Boardwalk). C invents a leash-length restriction. D overstates the restriction — dogs are permitted on two trails, no registration required.

mcq_questions.question_text: A family visits the park every other weekend from May to October. Would an annual pass save them money?

options.A: No, they would spend less paying the $15 daily rate for their visits
options.B: The cost is the same — 8 visits at $15 each equals $120
options.C: Yes, 13 visits at $15 each equals $195, much more than $120
options.D: The annual pass is not valid on weekends

mcq_questions.explanation: C is correct: May–October spans roughly 26 weekends; every other weekend = 13 visits × $15 = $195 vs. $120 for an annual pass — savings of $75. B is a calculation trap — 8 visits × $15 = $120, which would exactly break even, but 13 visits is the correct count over 26 weeks. A is incorrect at this visit frequency. D falsely restricts the annual pass to weekdays.

mcq_questions.question_text: When is the Visitor Centre open during winter months?

C overstates the closure — the centre is open on weekends in winter. D inverts the days — the winter schedule is weekend-only, not weekday-only.

mcq_questions.question_text: It rained heavily all morning. Which trail should visitors avoid?

options.B: Lakeshore Loop — flat trails become muddy and slippery in rain

B is a plausible-sounding trap — mud affects flat trails too, but only the Summit Trail carries an explicit wet-condition warning. C implies the boardwalk has a safety concern in rain, but no such restriction is listed. D invents an automatic closure policy.

diagram.text_description:
City of Maplewood — 2024 Waste & Recycling Collection Calendar:
Green Bin (organics): Weekly year-round. Zone A Monday, Zone B Tuesday. Food scraps, soiled paper, yard waste (max 4 bags, paper bags or open bin).
Blue Box (recycling): Every 2 weeks. Zone A alternating Mondays (from Jan 8), Zone B alternating Tuesdays (from Jan 9). Paper, cardboard, clean plastics #1–#5, glass, metal cans. NO plastic bags, Styrofoam, electronics.
Garbage: Every 2 weeks (opposite week from Blue Box). Max 3 bags/containers, each under 20 kg.
Bulky Items: By appointment — 905-555-0300 or online. $25/pickup, max 5 items. Mattresses, furniture, appliances. No hazardous materials.
Hazardous Waste: Depot only at 200 Industrial Rd, Sat 8 AM–4 PM. Paint, batteries, chemicals, motor oil, electronics.
Holiday: No collection on stat holidays — shifts to next business day.
Missed collection? Report by 8 PM same day at maplewood.ca/waste or 905-555-0300.

mcq_questions.question_text: Jana in Zone A had her Blue Box collected on January 8. When is her next Blue Box collection?

options.A: January 15 — one week later, matching the weekly Green Bin cycle
options.B: February 5 — four weeks after the first collection
options.C: January 22
options.D: January 9 — Zone B's first collection day

mcq_questions.explanation: C is correct: Blue Box is collected every two weeks; January 8 + 14 days = January 22. A (January 15) applies the weekly Green Bin frequency to the biweekly Blue Box — the most common error. B (February 5) applies a four-week interval. D (January 9) is Zone B's first collection, not Zone A's second.

mcq_questions.question_text: Tom wants to dispose of an old air conditioner and three cans of paint. How should he handle this?

options.A: Book a bulky item pickup for the AC; take paint to the hazardous waste depot
options.B: Place both items at the curb on garbage day — oversized items are accepted
options.C: Take both items to the hazardous waste depot on Saturday
options.D: Call for a bulky item pickup and include both items in the same order

mcq_questions.explanation: A is correct: the air conditioner is a bulky appliance (by-appointment, $25 pickup); paint is hazardous waste (depot only, 200 Industrial Rd, Sat 8 AM–4 PM). B is wrong — neither item belongs with regular garbage. C is a partial trap — paint belongs at the depot, but appliances are not accepted there (no hazardous materials). D fails because hazardous materials cannot be included in a bulky item pickup.

mcq_questions.question_text: A Zone B resident puts out 5 bags of yard waste on a Tuesday. What is the problem?

options.A: Yard waste is only collected during spring and fall seasons
options.B: Yard waste must go in the Blue Box, not the Green Bin
options.C: Zone B collects Green Bin on Mondays, not Tuesdays
options.D: The maximum is 4 bags per collection

mcq_questions.explanation: D is correct: the schedule allows yard waste in the Green Bin but limits it to 4 bags per collection — 5 bags exceeds this limit. A invents a seasonal restriction; the calendar states Green Bin is collected weekly year-round. B is false — yard waste belongs in the Green Bin, not the Blue Box. C is a data-swap trap: Zone B's Green Bin is collected on Tuesdays, not Mondays; Tuesday is correct for Zone B.

mcq_questions.question_text: Monday is a statutory holiday. When will Zone A's Green Bin be collected?

options.A: It will not be collected that week — Green Bin is skipped on holiday weeks
options.B: Tuesday — the next business day
options.C: The previous Friday — the city collects early before the holiday
options.D: The following Monday — collection resumes on the normal day

mcq_questions.explanation: B is correct: the calendar states 'No collection on statutory holidays — shifts to next business day.' For Zone A (Monday), the next business day after a Monday holiday is Tuesday. A invents a skip-week policy. C invents early collection, which the calendar does not mention. D is a common misconception — the shift is to the next day, not the following week's regular day.

mcq_questions.question_text: Sarah puts a Styrofoam container and old batteries in her Blue Box. Which items will be accepted?

options.A: Both — Styrofoam and batteries are clean recyclables
options.B: Neither — Styrofoam and electronics/batteries are excluded from Blue Box
options.C: The Styrofoam only — foam containers are accepted in Zone B
options.D: The batteries only — as long as they are removed from their packaging

mcq_questions.explanation: B is correct: the calendar explicitly lists 'NO plastic bags, Styrofoam, or electronics' for the Blue Box. Batteries fall under the electronics/hazardous category. A is the trap for readers who assume all packaging is recyclable. C invents a Zone B exception for Styrofoam that does not exist. D is false — batteries are not accepted in any Blue Box regardless of packaging.
